credit-card.notes: New York courts generally treat credit card debt as a written contract subject to the 6-year statute of limitations. However, the clock restarts with any payment or written acknowledgement. Important: as of April 2022, New York law bars collectors from suing on time-barred debts AND from making misrepresentations about time-barred debts.
credit-card.citation: N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 213(2); N.Y. Gen. Bus. Law § 601-a

medical.notes: Medical debt based on a written agreement is subject to New York's 6-year limitation. If based on an implied contract or account stated, it may also be 6 years. New York has significant medical debt protections — hospitals cannot report medical debt to credit bureaus for patients below 400% of the federal poverty level.

auto-loan.notes: Auto loan deficiency balances are treated as written contract claims, subject to New York's 6-year limitation period from the date of default or sale of repossessed vehicle.

personal-loan.notes: Personal loans evidenced by a written agreement are subject to the 6-year limitation period in New York.

student-loan.notes: Private student loans in New York are generally subject to the 6-year limitation for written contracts. Federal student loans are not subject to a statute of limitations for collection by the federal government, but private collectors may be limited.

mortgage-deficiency.notes: Mortgage deficiency judgments in New York must be sought within 90 days of the foreclosure sale (Real Property Actions and Proceedings Law § 1371). If a deficiency judgment has been obtained, it is then enforceable for 20 years.
mortgage-deficiency.citation: N.Y. Real Property Actions and Proceedings Law § 1371; N.Y. C.P.L.R. § 211
mortgage-deficiency.lawName: N.Y. Real Property Actions and Proceedings Law § 1371

utility-bill.notes: Utility bill debts are subject to New York's standard 6-year limitation period for written contracts.

regulatoryBody: New York State Department of Financial Services (DFS) & New York State Attorney General
complaints: File a complaint with the NY DFS at dfs.ny.gov or the NY AG at ag.ny.gov. New York's Debt Collection Procedures Law (Gen. Bus. Law § 601) provides additional state-level protections beyond the FDCPA.
